slides.title: What is Decision Theory?
slides.content:
A set of quantitative methods for reaching optimal decisions.
Is concerned with the reasoning underlying an agentвАЩs choices,
whether this is a mundane choice between taking the bus or
getting a taxi, or a more far-reaching choice about whether to
pursue a demanding political career.
Layman term: how we can decide what action should be taken
that could end up with the best result?
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recall: BayesвАЩ Rule
slides.content:
вАЬBayesianвАЭ: refer to inference methods that represent вАЬdegrees of
certaintyвАЭ using probability theory, and which leverage BayesвАЩ rule, to
update the degree of certainty given data.
Layman term: Update our belief based on prior belief and data so that we infer
something with a certain degree of uncertainty.
BayesвАЩ rule: a formula for computing the probability distribution over
possible values of an unknown quantity рЭРї given observed data рЭСМ = рЭС¶.
рЭСЭ рЭРї = вДО рЭСЭ(рЭСМ = рЭС¶|рЭРї = вДО)
рЭСЭ рЭРї = вДО рЭСМ = рЭС¶ =
рЭСЭ(рЭСМ = рЭС¶)
Follows the product rule of probability: рЭСЭ вДО рЭС¶ рЭСЭ рЭС¶ = рЭСЭ вДО рЭСЭ рЭС¶ вДО = рЭСЭ(вДО, рЭС¶)
рЭСЭ рЭРї = вДО рЭСМ = рЭС¶ : posterior distribution / conditional distribution.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recall: BayesвАЩ Rule: in Detail
slides.content:
рЭСЭ рЭРї = вДО рЭСЭ(рЭСМ = рЭС¶|рЭРї = вДО)
рЭСЭ рЭРї = вДО рЭСМ = рЭС¶ =
рЭСЭ(рЭСМ = рЭС¶)
рЭСЭ(рЭРї): what we know about possible values of рЭРї before we see any data вАУ
the prior distribution (layman terms: prior belief).
рЭСЭ(рЭСМ|рЭРї = вДО): the distribution over possible outcomes рЭСМ we expect to see if
рЭРї = вДО вАУ the likelihood (layman terms: the observed data).
Multiplying the prior distribution and the likelihood C unnormalized joint
distribution рЭСЭ(рЭРї = вДО, рЭСМ = рЭС¶). Convert into a normalized distribution by
dividing by рЭСЭ(рЭСМ = рЭС¶), which is denoted as the marginal likelihood.
рЭСЭ(рЭРї = вДО|рЭСМ = рЭС¶): represents our new belief state (updated belief) about
the possible values of рЭРї вАУ the posterior distribution.
posterior вИЭ prior √Ч likelihood

slides.title: (Bayesian) Decision Theory: in Detail
slides.content:
In decision theory, we assume the decision maker, or agent, has a set of
possible actions, рЭТЬ, to choose from.
Example: doctor treat someone who may have cancer, actions рЭСО вИИ рЭТЬ:
Do nothing;
Give patient expensive drugs with bad side effect but can save their life;
We can compute (implicitly) the cost of treating a person (test = 0: cancer
negative; age = 0: young age; action = 1: apply the drugs).
cancer
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

cancer
Every action рЭСО вИИ рЭТЬ has cost and benefits, depending on the underlying
state of nature вДО вИИ вДЛ. How can we explicitly model such cost / benefit?
Define: loss function вДУ(вДО, рЭСО) вАУ loss incurred if action рЭСО is taken at state вДО.
The next question: how to leverage this loss?
Equivalently: how can we actually know quantitatively what is the best action if we
compute the loss function?
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Define: loss function вДУ(вДО, рЭСО) вАУ action рЭСО taken at state вДО.
Compute a posterior expected loss (risk) for each possible рЭСО: (where the
data is defined as рЭС•)
рЭЬМ рЭСО рЭС• вЙЬ рЭФЉ вДУ вДО, рЭСО = аЈН вДУ вДО, рЭСО рЭСЭ(вДО|рЭС•)
вДО рЭС•
рЭСЭ
вДОвИИвДЛ
вИЧ
We thus define the optimal policy рЭЬЛ рЭС• , which is also called the Bayes
вИЧ
estimator or Bayes decision rule рЭЫњ (рЭС•):
вИЧ
рЭЬЛ рЭС• = arg min рЭЬМ рЭСО рЭС• = arg min рЭФЉ вДУ вДО, рЭСО
вДО рЭС•

рЭСОвИИрЭТЬ рЭСОвИИрЭТЬ

slides.content:

The optimal policy рЭЬЛ рЭС• , or Bayes decision rule рЭЫњ (рЭС•):

рЭЬЛ рЭС• = arg min рЭЬМ рЭСО рЭС• = arg min рЭФЉ вДУ вДО, рЭСО

This is defined as the maximum expected utility principle.

predict given an observed input рЭС• вИИ рЭТ≥.

We may be able to say вАЬI donвАЩt knowвАЭ instead of returning an
answer that we donвАЩt really trust вАУ choosing the reject option.
Important in domains such as medicine where we may be risk adverse.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P18EdAKuC1U

slides.title: Plotting the ROC Curve: Example
slides.content:
ROC curve вАУ Plot the TPR vs FPR as an implicit function of рЭЬП, i.e., plot
TPR vs FPR with varying рЭЬП.
How do we compare different ROC curves?
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The ROC Curve
slides.content:
Quality of a ROC curve is often summarized using the Area
Under the Curve (AUC). Higher AUC scores better; max is 1.
Another statistic is the Equal Error
Rate (EER), or the cross-over rate
Defined as the value which satisfies
рЭРєрЭСГрЭСЕ = рЭРєрЭСБрЭСЕ = 1 вИТ рЭСЗрЭСГрЭСЕ.
Compute the EER by drawing a line from
the top left to the bottom right and seeing
where it intersects the ROC curve.
Lower EER scores are better; the
minimum is obviously 0 (top left corner).
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Class Imbalance and the ROC Curve
slides.content:
In some problems, there is severe class imbalance.
E.g., in information retrieval, the set of negatives (irrelevant items) is
usually much larger than the set of positives (relevant items).
Discuss: would the ROC curve be affected by class imbalance?
Discuss: how would class imbalance affect the effectiveness of ROC?
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Precision-Recall Curve
slides.content:
Problems of the ROC curve:
Require well-defined вАЬtrue positivesвАЭ and вАЬtrue negativesвАЭ;
However, in some problems, вАЬnegativeвАЭ may not be well-defined.
E.g., detecting objects in images: if the detector works by classifying
patches, then the # of patches examined вАФ the # of true negatives вАФ
is a param of the algorithm, instead of the problem definition.
Can we evaluate the classification computed just from positives?
Precision and the precision-recall curve:
Precision: replace the FPR with a quantity that is computed just from
positives.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Precision-Recall (PR) Curve
slides.content:
Precision: fraction of our detections are actually positive:
рЭТЂ рЭЬП вЙЬ рЭСЭ рЭС¶ = 1 рЭС¶аЈЬ = 1, рЭЬП = рЭСЗрЭСГ /(рЭСЗрЭСГ + рЭРєрЭСГ )
рЭЬП рЭЬП рЭЬП
Recall (TPR): fraction of the positives we actually detected:
вДЫ рЭЬП вЙЬ рЭСЭ рЭС¶аЈЬ = 1 рЭС¶ = 1, рЭЬП = рЭСЗрЭСГ /(рЭСЗрЭСГ + рЭРєрЭСБ )
рЭЬП рЭЬП рЭЬП
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: An Example of PR Curve in Object Detection
slides.content:
Run object detector on all test images (with NMS).
For each category, compute Average Precision
(AP) = area under Precision vs Recall Curve.
For each detection (highest score to lowest score):
If it matches some GT box with loU > 0.5, mark it as
positive and eliminate the GT.
Otherwise mark it as negative.
Plot a point on PR Curve.
Average Precision (AP) = area under PR curve
Mean Average Precision (mAP) = average of AP
for each category.
For "COCO mAP": Compute mAP@thresh for
each loU threshold (0.5, 0.55, 0.6, ..., 0.95) and
take average.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Plotting the PR Curve: Example
slides.content:
PR curve вАУ Plot the precision vs recall as an implicit function of рЭЬП, i.e., plot
precision vs recall with varying рЭЬП.
How do we compare different PR curves?
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The PR Curve
slides.content:
The statistics to summarize the PR curve:
Precision at рЭРЊ score: the precision for a fixed recall level;
AUC: the area under the PR curve;
Interpolated precision:
Possible precision does not drop monotonically with
recall. E.g., a classifier has 90% precision at 10% recall,
and 96% precision at 20% recall.
Measure the maximum precision we can achieve with at
least a recall of 10% (which would be 96%).
Average precision: avg of interpolated precisions
Equal to the area under the interpolated PR curve.
The mean average precision (mAP): the mean of the AP
over a set of different PR curves.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The PR Curve and F-scores
slides.content:
For a given point on the PR curve, can combine
the precision value рЭТЂ and the recall value вДЫ into a
single statistics:
2
1 1 1 рЭЫљ 1
= +
рЭРє 1 + рЭЫљ2 рЭТЂ 1 + рЭЫљ2 вДЫ
рЭЫљ
If we set рЭЫљ = 1, then we get the harmonic mean of
the precision and recall, known as the рЭРє score.
1
1 1 1 1
= +
рЭРє 2 рЭТЂ рЭСЕ
1
Questions:
Why do we use the вАЬharmonic meanвАЭ instead of the
вАЬarithmetic meanвАЭ (рЭТЂ + вДЫ)/2?
Would the PR curve be affected by class imbalance?
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Probabilistic Prediction Problem
slides.content:
In previous slides: assumed the set of possible actions was to pick a
single class label (or possibly the вАЬrejectвАЭ action).
More often: the set of possible actions is to pick a probability
distribution over some value of interest:
Perform probabilistic prediction, rather than predicting a specific value.
For Classification: states of nature вЖТ class labels: вДЛ = рЭТі = {1, вА¶ , рЭРґ};
actions вЖТ class labels: рЭТЬ = рЭТі.
For Probabilistic prediction: states of nature вЖТ вДО = рЭСЭ(рЭСМ|рЭС•); action вЖТ
рЭСО = рЭСЮ рЭСМ рЭС• .
What we want: to pick рЭСЮ to minimizeрЭФЉ вДУ вДО, рЭСО = рЭФЉ[вДУ(рЭСЭ, рЭСЮ)] for a given рЭС•.
The key: how to define the loss function вДУ(рЭСЭ, рЭСЮ)?
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Decision Theory and Classifiers:
slides.content:
вЬУ KL-Divergence
вЬУ Entropy and Mutual Information
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: KL Divergence (KL-Div)
slides.content:
Loss function is measured between two вАЬdistributionsвАЭ.
Kullback Leibler divergence (KL divergence) is a measure of how
one probability distribution is different from a second:
вИЧ
рЭСЭ рЭС¶
вИЧ
рЭФї рЭСЭ вИ• рЭСЮ вЙЬ аЈН рЭСЭ рЭС¶ log
рЭРЊрЭРњ
рЭСЮ рЭС¶аЈЬ
вИЧ
рЭС¶ вИИрЭТі
Here рЭСЭ is the true distribution and рЭСЮ is the predicted distribution.
KL divergence can be further expanded as:
вИЧ вИЧ вИЧ
рЭФї рЭСЭ вИ• рЭСЮ = ѕГ рЭСЭ рЭС¶ log рЭСЭ(рЭС¶ ) вИТ ѕГ рЭСЭ рЭС¶ log рЭСЮ рЭС¶аЈЬ = вИТвДН рЭСЭ +
рЭРЊрЭРњ
вДН (рЭСЭ, рЭСЮ). вДН(рЭСЭ): entropy of рЭСЭ; вДН : cross entropy between рЭСЭ and рЭСЮ.
рЭСРрЭСТ рЭСРрЭСТ
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Entropy
slides.content:
The key component of KL Divergence: entropy.
The entropy of a probability distribution can be interpreted as a
measure of uncertainty, or lack of predictability, associated with a
random variable drawn from a given distribution.
Entropy: define the information content of a data source.
Suppose we observe a sequence of symbols рЭСЛ ~ рЭСЭ generated from
рЭСЫ
distribution рЭСЭ. What does it mean by рЭСЭ having high / low entropy?
If рЭСЭ has high entropy, hard to predict the value of each observation рЭСЛ .
рЭСЫ
Hence dataset рЭТЯ = рЭСЛ , вА¶ , рЭСЛ has high information content.
1 рЭСЫ
If рЭСЭ is a degenerate distribution with 0 entropy, then every рЭСЛ will be the
рЭСЫ
same, so рЭТЯ does not contain much information.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Entropy for Discrete Random Variables
slides.content:
More specifically, formulate the entropy of a discrete RV рЭСЛ with
distribution рЭСЭ over рЭРЊ states (classes) as:
рЭРЊ
вДН рЭСЛ вЙЬ вИТ аЈН рЭСЭ рЭСЛ = рЭСШ log рЭСЭ рЭСЛ = рЭСШ = вИТрЭФЉ log рЭСЭ рЭСЛ
2 рЭСЛ
рЭСШ
Note that вДН рЭСЛ is equivalent to the notation of вДН рЭСЭ .
Question: when will the distribution obtain the Max / Min Entropy?

вДН рЭСЛ вЙЬ вИТ аЈН рЭСЭ рЭСЛ = рЭСШ log рЭСЭ рЭСЛ = рЭСШ = вИТрЭФЉ log рЭСЭ рЭСЛ
2 рЭСЛ
Discrete distribution with Maximum entropy is uniform distribution.
For a рЭРЊ-ary RV, the entropy is max-ed if рЭСЭ(рЭС• = рЭСШ) = 1/рЭРЊ; рЭРї рЭСЛ = log рЭРЊ.

mass on one state

1) = рЭЬГ and рЭСЭ(рЭСЛ = 0) = 1 вИТ рЭЬГ:

0) log рЭСЭ рЭСЛ = 0 ]

slides.title: Mutual Information
slides.content:
KL divergence: measure how dissimilar two distributions were.
Then can we measure how dependent two RVs are?
Turn the question of measuring the dependence of two RVs into a
question about the similarity of their distributions.
The notion of Mutual Information (MI) btw two RVs, defined as:
рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ вЙЬ рЭФї рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶ вИ• рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭСЭ рЭС¶ = аЈН аЈН рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶ log
рЭРЊрЭРњ
рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭСЭ(рЭС¶)
рЭС¶ рЭС•
Note: the notation is defined as рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ as рЭСЛ and/or рЭСМ can represent a
set of variable вАУ рЭХА(рЭСЛ; рЭСМ, рЭСН) is the MI between рЭСЛ and рЭСМ, рЭСН .
MI is always non-negative: рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ вЙ• 0. Reach the bound of 0 iff
рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶ = рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭСЭ рЭС¶ .
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Mutual Information: Interpretation
slides.content:
рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ вЙЬ рЭФї рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶ вИ• рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭСЭ рЭС¶ = аЈН аЈН рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶ log
рЭРЊрЭРњ
рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭСЭ(рЭС¶)
рЭС¶ рЭС•
MI is a KL-Div between the joint and factored marginal distributions:
MI measures the information gain if we update from a model that treats
the two variables as independent рЭСЭ(рЭС•)рЭСЭ(рЭС¶) to one that models their true
joint density рЭСЭ(рЭС•, рЭС¶).
To gain further, re-express MI in terms of joint and conditional entropies:
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ = вДН рЭСЛ вИТ вДН рЭСЛ|рЭСМ = вДН рЭСМ вИТ вДН рЭСМ|рЭСЛ , вДН рЭСМ|рЭСЛ = вДН рЭСЛ, рЭСМ вИТ рЭРї(рЭСЛ)
MI between рЭСЛ and рЭСМ is thus expressed as the reduction in uncertainty
about рЭСЛ after observing рЭСМ, or, by symmetry, the reduction in uncertainty
about рЭСМ after observing рЭСЛ.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ вЙЬ рЭФї рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶ вИ• рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭСЭ рЭС¶ = аЈН аЈН рЭСЭ рЭС•, рЭС¶ log
рЭРЊрЭРњ
рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭСЭ(рЭС¶)
рЭС¶ рЭС•
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ = вДН рЭСЛ вИТ вДН рЭСЛ|рЭСМ = вДН рЭСМ вИТ вДН рЭСМ|рЭСЛ , вДН рЭСМ|рЭСЛ = вДН рЭСЛ, рЭСМ вИТ рЭРї(рЭСЛ)
An alternative proof that conditioning, on average, reduces entropy!
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ = вДН рЭСЛ вИТ вДН рЭСЛ|рЭСМ вЙ• 0 вЖТ вДН рЭСЛ|рЭСМ вЙ§ вДН рЭСЛ
One can also obtain the following equivalent results of MI:
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ = вДН рЭСЛ, рЭСМ вИТ вДН рЭСЛ|рЭСМ вИТ вДН рЭСМ|рЭСЛ
рЭХА рЭСЛ; рЭСМ = вДН рЭСЛ + вДН рЭСМ вИТ вДН рЭСЛ, рЭСМ
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Decision Theory and Classifiers:
slides.content:
вЬУ The Frequentist Decision
вЬУ Application in the Context of Supervised Learning
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recall: Frequentist vs. Bayesian
slides.content:
Frequentist interpretation: probabilities represent long run
frequencies of events that can happen multiple times.
E.g., Flipping the coin many times, we expect it to land heads
about half the time.
Bayesian interpretation: quantify uncertainty or ignorance
about something
E.g., The coin is equally likely to land heads/tails on next toss.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Frequentist Decision Theory
slides.content:
In the Frequentist decision theory, treat the unknown state
of nature (denoted by рЭЬљ instead of вДО) as a fixed yet unknown
quantity, and treat the data рЭС• as random.
Instead of conditioning over рЭС•, we average over it to compute the loss
expect to incur if apply our estimator to different datasets.
Define the frequentist risk of an estimator рЭЫњ given an unknown
state of nature рЭЬљ as the expected loss when applying рЭЫњ to data
рЭС•, where the expectation is over the data, sampled from рЭСЭ(рЭС•|рЭЬљ):
рЭСЕ рЭЬљ, рЭЫњ вЙЬ рЭФЉ вДУ рЭЬљ, рЭЫњ рЭС•
рЭС• рЭЬљ
рЭСЭ
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.content:
рЭСЕ рЭЬљ, рЭЫњ вЙЬ рЭФЉ вДУ рЭЬГ, рЭЫњ рЭС•
рЭС• рЭЬљ
рЭСЭ
In general, the true state of nature рЭЬљ that generates the data рЭС•
is unknown, so cannot compute the risk directly. Then how?
One solution: to assume a prior рЭЬЛ for рЭЬГ then average it out вАУ
0
the Bayes Risk (integrated risk):
рЭСЕ рЭЫњ вЙЬ рЭФЉ рЭСЕ рЭЬљ, рЭЫњ = аґ± рЭССрЭЬљрЭССрЭС•рЭЬЛ рЭЬљ рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭЬљ вДУ рЭЬљ, рЭЫњ рЭС•
рЭЬЛ рЭЬЛ 0
0 0 рЭЬљ
Decision rule that minimize the Bayes risk: Bayes estimator.

slides.title: The Bayes Estimator
slides.content:
Recall: in the Bayesian decision theory, we define the optimal
policy, also the Bayes estimator or Bayes decision rule as:
вИЧ
рЭЬЛ рЭС• = arg min рЭЬМ рЭСО рЭС• = arg min рЭФЉ вДУ вДО, рЭСО
вДО рЭС•
рЭСЭ
рЭСОвИИрЭТЬ рЭСОвИИрЭТЬ
In the Frequentist decision theory, the optimal estimator is:
рЭЫњ рЭС• = arg min аґ± рЭССрЭЬГрЭЬЛ рЭЬГ рЭСЭ рЭС• рЭЬГ вДУ(рЭЬГ, рЭСО) вЙ° arg min аґ± рЭССрЭЬГрЭСЭ рЭЬГ рЭС• вДУ(рЭЬГ, рЭСО)
0
рЭСО рЭСО
The two forms are actually equivalent! What does this mean?
Bayesian approach provides a good way of achieving the frequentist
goal вАУ picking the optimal action case-by-case is optimal on average!
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.content:
Use of a prior might seem undesirable in certain cases. We can

The minimax estimators are overly
conservative!

slides.title: ERM and Frequentist Decision Theory
slides.content:
We consider how to apply the frequentist decision theory in the
context of supervised learning.
The risk: рЭСЕ рЭЬљ, рЭЫњ вЙЬ рЭФЉ вДУ рЭЬљ, рЭЫњ рЭС• .
рЭС• рЭЬљ

вИЧ
of some model рЭЬљ .
Meanwhile, the discussion w.r.t. data within a dataset рЭС• вИИ рЭТЯ, the risk
can be rewritten:
вИЧ вИЧ
рЭСЕ рЭЫњ, рЭЬљ = рЭФЉ вДУ рЭЬљ , рЭЫњ рЭТЯ
вИЧ

In supervised learning,
Different unknown state of nature (output рЭС¶) for each different input рЭС•,
Estimator рЭЫњ is a prediction function рЭС¶аЈЬ = рЭСУ(рЭС•),
вИЧ вИЧ
The state of nature (as a whole) is the true distribution рЭСЭ (рЭС•, рЭС¶ ),

Thus, the risk of an estimator is (in terms of рЭСУ and рЭСЭ ) is:

slides.title: Regularization and Regularized Risk
slides.content:
To avoid the chance of overfitting, it is common to add a
complexity penalty to the objective function вАУ regularization.
With regularization, we obtain the regularized empirical risk:
рЭСЕ рЭСУ, рЭТЯ = рЭСЕ рЭСУ, рЭРЈ + рЭЬЖрЭРґ(рЭСУ)
рЭЬЖ
рЭРґ(рЭСУ) measures the complexity of the prediction function рЭСУ and рЭЬЖ вЙ• 0 is
the hyperparameter that controls the strength of complexity penalty.
In practice, the regularization is often applied to the model parameters
directly, thus the regularized ER is rewritten: рЭСЕ рЭЬГ, рЭТЯ = рЭСЕ рЭЬГ, рЭРЈ + рЭЬЖрЭРґ(рЭЬГ).
рЭЬЖ
If the loss function is a log loss term, while the regularizer is a negative
1
рЭСБ
log prior, рЭСЕ рЭЬГ, рЭТЯ = вИТ ѕГ log рЭСЭ рЭС¶ рЭС• , рЭЬГ вИТ рЭЬЖ log рЭСЭ рЭЬГ . Minimizing this
рЭЬЖ рЭСЫ=1 рЭСЫ рЭСЫ
рЭСБ
is exactly equivalent to the MAP estimation.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: OccamвАЩs Razor
slides.content:
Occam's razor is the problem-solving principle that recommends
searching for explanations constructed with the smallest possible
set of elements.
Also known as the principle of parsimony or the law of parsimony.
Consider 2 models, a simple one, рЭСЪ , and a more complex one, рЭСЪ .
1 2
Suppose both can explain the data by suitably optimizing their
аЈ° аЈ°
parameters, i.e., рЭСЭ(рЭТЯ|рЭЬљ , рЭСЪ ) and рЭСЭ(рЭТЯ|рЭЬљ , рЭСЪ ) are both large.
1 1 2 2
Intuitively should prefer рЭСЪ , as it is simpler and as good as рЭСЪ .
1 2
In regularization, it corresponds to the complexity penalty, where
more complex models would result in a larger regularization term.
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Population Risk Est. with Validation
slides.content:
A simple way to estimate the population risk for a supervised
learning setup: cross-validation.
Partition the dataset into two, one used for training models (training set),
another, the validation set or holdout set, used for assessing the risk.
Note that in supervised learning, the real вАЬtest dataвАЭ are inaccessible.
Process: fit the model on the training set, use its performance
on the validation set as an approx. to the population risk:
Make the dependence of the рЭСЕ (рЭСУ, рЭТЯ) on the dataset more explicit:
рЭЬЖ
1
рЭСЕ рЭЬљ, рЭТЯ = аЈН вДУ рЭС¶, рЭСУ рЭС•; рЭЬљ + рЭЬЖрЭРґ(рЭЬљ)
рЭЬЖ
рЭРЈ
рЭС•,рЭС¶ вИИрЭТЯ
аЈ°
Also define рЭЬљ рЭТЯ = arg min рЭСЕ рЭЬГ, рЭТЯ , рЭТЯ and рЭТЯ are partitions.
рЭЬЖ рЭЬЖ рЭС°рЭСЯ рЭС£рЭСОрЭСЩ
рЭЬГ
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Cross-Validation
slides.content:
аЈ°
For each model, we fit it to the training set to obtain рЭЬљ рЭТЯ . Then use the
рЭЬЖ рЭС°рЭСЯ
unregularized empirical risk on the validation set as an estimate of the
рЭС£рЭСОрЭСЩ аЈ°
population risk вАУ validation risk: рЭСЕ вЙЬ рЭСЕ рЭЬљ рЭТЯ , рЭТЯ .
рЭЬЖ 0 рЭЬЖ рЭС°рЭСЯ рЭС£рЭСОрЭСЩ
Any shortcomings of this method?
Consider the training data is very limited вАУ not enough data to train on!
Impossible to make reliable estimate of future performance.
Solution?
Cross-validation!
¬© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Split the training data into рЭРЊ folds;
For each fold рЭСШ вИИ 1, вА¶ , рЭРЊ , train on
all but ONE fold, in a round-robin
fashion (in turns, circled around all
possible folds).
Formally, the cross-validation risk is formulated as:
1
рЭСРрЭС£ рЭРЊ аЈ°
рЭСЕ вЙЬ ѕГ рЭСЕ рЭЬљ рЭТЯ , рЭТЯ
рЭЬЖ рЭСШ=1 0 рЭЬЖ вИТрЭСШ рЭСШ
рЭРЊ
рЭТЯ is the data in the рЭСШ-th fold, and рЭТЯ is all the other data. If set рЭРЊ = рЭСБ,
рЭСШ вИТрЭСШ
the method is known as leave-one-out cross-validation.
